name: Western Galician

ancestors.notes: Standard/Central Galician, the immediate parent variety.

Lusitanian or pre-Celtic Atlantic coastal substrate: the gheada has been linked to a possible substrate influence in the Atlantic coastal zone of Galicia-Portugal, where the pharyngeal/glottal fricative may have old roots.
Celtic substrate, somewhat stronger in the coastal western zone.

notes: Western Galician (galego occidental), spoken in Pontevedra province and the coastal Atlantic zone of A Coruña. KEY FEATURE — GHEADA: the defining hallmark of western Galician, a substitution of /ɡ/ by a voiceless fricative [h] (often described as glottal [h], pharyngeal [ħ], or sometimes velar [x]): 'galego' → [haˈlɛho], 'gato' → [ˈhato]. The gheada affects: word-initial /ɡ/, intervocalic /ɡ/; the ⟨gu⟩ digraph before e/i typically retains /ɡ/. The origin of the gheada is debated: substrate hypothesis (Atlantic pre-Celtic or Lusitanian fricative), internal development from Galaico-Portuguese /ɡ/ spirantisation, or Portuguese influence. The standard RAG norm does NOT accept gheada in formal writing, but the RAG acknowledges it as a legitimate dialectal feature. NOTE: gheada speakers still write ⟨g⟩ in the standard orthography — the feature is purely phonological. Sources: Fernández Rei (1990) *Dialectoloxía da lingua galega*, Dubert García (2006), Álvarez & Xove (2002).

location.notes: Pontevedra — a representative locality of the western Galician block (Pontevedra province and coastal A Coruña), the gheada heartland. [Wikidata Q12411 P625]
